subject: maths
title: Digit Reversal
question: I am thinking of a two-digit number. If you reverse its digits, you get a number that is 27 more than my original number. Also, the two digits add up to 11. What is my number?
modelReasoningPath:
The tens digit and units digit add to 11. Reversing the number increases it by 27. If the digits are a and b, then 10b + a − (10a + b) = 27, so 9b − 9a = 27, meaning b − a = 3. Combined with a + b = 11: b = 7, a = 4. The number is 47 (reversed: 74, difference: 27 ✓).

What to listen for: A child using algebra is impressive but not required. More interesting is whether they reason: "the digits differ by 3 and add to 11 - so they must be 4 and 7." Trial and improvement is fine, but look for systematic checking rather than random guessing. ---

subject: maths
title: Sharing Remainders
question: Four friends are dividing a pile of sweets equally, but there are 2 left over. A fifth friend then arrives, and when they share equally between five people, there are 3 left over. What is the smallest number of sweets the pile could contain?
modelReasoningPath:
Find the smallest number that leaves remainder 2 when divided by 4, and remainder 3 when divided by 5. Numbers leaving remainder 2 ÷ 4: 2, 6, 10, 14, 18, 22… Numbers leaving remainder 3 ÷ 5: 3, 8, 13, 18, 23… First number in both lists: 18.

What to listen for: A child who writes out both lists and looks for overlap is thinking well. Watch whether they understand what a remainder is - some children confuse it with the quotient. Asking "can you check your answer?" is useful here. ---

subject: maths
title: The Persistent Adder
question: Starting from 1, I keep adding the next odd number each time. So I have: 1, then 1 + 3 = 4, then 4 + 5 = 9, then 9 + 7 = 16, and so on. After I have added the 20th odd number, what is my total?
modelReasoningPath:
The sum of the first n odd numbers always equals n². The sequence 1, 4, 9, 16… are perfect squares. After 20 odd numbers: 20² = 400. (The 20th odd number is 2 × 20 − 1 = 39.)

What to listen for: Does the child spot the pattern (1, 4, 9, 16 are perfect squares) from the examples given in the question? Can they generalise? A strong candidate notices the pattern immediately; a good candidate spots it after writing out a few more terms. Credit anyone who sees it, even if they can't explain why it works. ---

subject: maths
title: The Sale Price Trap
question: A coat normally costs £80. In a sale, the price is reduced by 20%. After the sale ends, the shop puts the price back up by 25%. What is the new price? Is it more than, less than, or the same as the original £80?
answer: Exactly £80 - the same as the original.
modelReasoningPath:
20% off: £80 × 0.8 = £64. Then 25% on: £64 × 1.25 = £80.

What to listen for: Almost every child says "they should cancel but I'm not sure which way." Very many say the final price is more (because 25 > 20). The insight is that the 25% is applied to a smaller number. A child who says "you can't just add percentages because they're of different amounts" is thinking at an excellent level. ---

subject: maths
title: The Fuel Tank
question: My car's fuel tank is three-quarters full. During a long drive, I use two-thirds of the fuel that was in the tank at the start of the journey. What fraction of a full tank do I have left?
modelReasoningPath:
Start with 3/4 of a tank. Use 2/3 of that: 2/3 × 3/4 = 6/12 = 1/2. Remaining: 3/4 − 1/2 = 3/4 − 2/4 = 1/4.

What to listen for: The key distinction is "two-thirds of what is in the tank" versus "two-thirds of the full tank." A child who computes 2/3 of the full tank (giving 2/3 used, leaving 1/4... wait, that gives 1/3 − something, which doesn't parse) has misread the question. Ask them to re-read it slowly. A child who multiplies fractions correctly and then subtracts is handling this well. --- ## SECTION B - Estimation & Order of Magnitude Questions that test whether a child can reason sensibly about size and scale, making and justifying approximations. ---
